name: Core i3-12100F
description: The best budget CPU Intel ever made. 4 P-cores with Hyperthreading, no E-cores. The 'F' means no integrated graphics — needs a discrete GPU. Competes with and often beats Ryzen 5 5600 at lower price. Runs extremely cool and quiet on a cheap air cooler. Perfect for a first gaming PC build under ₹50,000.

name: Core i5-12400F
description: One of the greatest value gaming CPUs of all time. 6 P-cores + 12 threads hits the sweet spot for gaming. The 65W base TDP means it runs cool on a stock cooler. Paired with an RX 6600 or RTX 3060, this is a complete 1080p gaming rig. Still relevant years after launch — games don't need more than 6 strong cores for most titles.

name: Core i5-12600K
description: The chip that defined Alder Lake's success. First mainstream hybrid architecture — 6 P-cores for gaming and heavy tasks, 4 E-cores for background work. Absolutely destroyed Ryzen 5 5600X at launch in gaming benchmarks. The 'K' means unlocked multiplier for overclocking. Needs Z690/Z790 board for OC. Intel UHD 770 iGPU means you can run displays even without a GPU.

name: Core i7-12700K
description: The sweet spot of Alder Lake for creators who also game. 8 P-cores handle Premiere, DaVinci, Blender while 4 E-cores manage Discord, Chrome, background tasks. Single-core performance nearly matches the i9. If you render videos and play games on the same machine, the i7-12700K is the rational choice over the i9's premium pricing.
pros: Best multi-task chip of its tier

name: Core i9-12900K
description: The fastest gaming CPU in early 2022 — briefly. Runs extremely hot (241W PL2) requiring a 360mm AIO as minimum. 8P+8E cores make it a powerhouse for creative work. Overclocked it was the world's fastest single-threaded CPU at the time. The i9-12900KS (Special Edition) pushed this to 5.5 GHz out of the box. Power consumption and heat are the real costs.

name: Core i3-13100F
description: Marginally faster than i3-12100F with slight clock speed bump. Same 4-core 8-thread design. Ideal for a first PC or upgrading an old system on a tight budget. Remarkable that Intel offers this level of performance at this price — it beats CPUs that cost 2× more just 3 years earlier.
cons: 4 cores will show limitations in newer games

name: Core i5-13400F
description: Possibly the best value mainstream gaming CPU ever released. Gained E-cores over the 12400F, pushing multi-threaded performance significantly. Gaming FPS nearly matches far more expensive chips. Paired with DDR4 on a B760 board, a complete gaming system can be built for ₹60,000–70,000. Still one of our top recommendations in 2024–2025.

name: Core i5-13600K
description: A massive jump over its predecessor. 6 P-cores + 8 E-cores = 14 cores total. The E-core count doubling from 12th Gen changes multi-threaded scores dramatically. In Cinebench R23 multi, it rivals the i9-12900K. Best mid-range overclocker — with Z790 and DDR5-7200, it punches well above its price. Extremely popular in gaming + content creation setups.
cons: DDR5 cost if going that route

name: Core i7-13700K
description: The professional creator's daily driver. 8P+8E cores, 24 threads. In content creation benchmarks it often matches the i9-12900K while consuming similar power. The 5.4 GHz boost clock is ferocious. A 360mm AIO is recommended. Excellent for software developers running VMs, Docker containers, and compilation jobs alongside gaming.

name: Core i9-13900K
description: The fastest gaming CPU money could buy for much of 2023 — until AMD's 3D V-Cache chips arrived. 8P+16E = 24 cores total with 32 threads. 5.8 GHz max boost. Dominated Cinebench R23 multi. Runs extremely hot. Delidding (removing heat spreader) and replacing with liquid metal drops temps 20°C. World record overclock: 9.008 GHz on LN2. The pinnacle of Raptor Lake.

name: Core i9-13900KS
description: The Special Edition variant — Intel's highest binned 13th Gen chips. Achieves 6.0 GHz all-core boost out of the box, the first consumer CPU to do so officially. Every chip is hand-tested and binned for maximum performance. Only meaningful if you need the absolute ceiling. At ₹60,000 it's expensive for incremental gains over the i9-13900K.
cons: Not meaningfully faster in games vs 13900K

name: Core i5-14400F
description: Effectively an i5-13400F with a tiny clock bump. Same platform, same board compatibility. The 2024 budget gaming champion alongside AMD's competing offerings. If you find it cheaper than the 13400F, buy this one. If the 13400F is cheaper, buy that. They're functionally identical for gaming.
cons: Raptor Lake instability concerns (fixed via BIOS)

name: Core i5-14600K
description: Small boost over i5-13600K. 5.3 GHz boost vs 5.1 GHz. Same core config, same power. Overclocked on DDR5-7200+, this is a beast for gaming. If you're on an older Z690/Z790 board already, a BIOS update drops this right in. One of the last chips for LGA1700.

name: Core i7-14700K
description: The i7-14700K gained 4 extra E-cores over its predecessor (12E vs 8E = 20 cores total, 28 threads). This pushed it ahead in multi-threaded workloads significantly. A great chip for running Docker, VS Code, gaming, and video export simultaneously. The 253W PL2 is the thermal reality check — budget for a 360mm AIO.

name: Core i9-14900K
description: Peak of the Raptor Lake architecture — matches 13900KS base performance at same price as 13900K. 6.0 GHz boost standard. Suffered from WHEA errors and instability under extreme loads in 2024 — Intel acknowledged this was a microcode issue and released a patch (0x129). Fully patched systems are stable. Still the fastest LGA1700 chip available.

name: Core i9-14900KS
description: The absolute pinnacle of LGA1700. 6.2 GHz boost, highest-binned silicon in the entire Raptor Lake lineup. Essentially pointless for everyday use — the gaming FPS difference over i9-13900K is 1–3%. This is an enthusiast trophy chip. Collectors and benchmarkers love it. If you need to justify it, you don't need it.
cons: Insane price premium for marginal gain

name: Core Ultra 5 125H
description: Entry to the Meteor Lake family. The 2 Low-Power E-cores mean the chip can handle background music, notifications, and idle tasks at near-zero power. Intel Arc iGPU is a real step up from Iris Xe — can handle casual gaming at 1080p low-medium. NPU enables basic AI features. Great battery life in thin laptops like Lenovo Slim 5i.

name: Core Ultra 7 165H
description: The mainstream high-end Meteor Lake chip. Found in Dell XPS 15 (2024), ASUS Zenbook Pro 14. Strong all-day performance. The 16-core count helps with compilation and Docker. Gaming is respectable but behind AMD Strix Point. The NPU is a real feature here — runs local Whisper (speech-to-text) and Stable Diffusion XS inference.
cons: AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX wins on GPU performance
Intel Arc drivers can be inconsistent

name: Core Ultra 9 185H
description: Top Meteor Lake chip for the high-performance laptop segment. Found in Razer Blade 14 (2024), ASUS ProArt Studiobook. The 115W turbo boost makes it blistering for short bursts — useful in rendering and compilation sprints. Intel Arc handles DaVinci Resolve GPU acceleration respectably.

name: Core Ultra 5 245K
description: Arrow Lake's mid-range offering. No Hyperthreading means 14 cores = 14 threads — a step back in multi-threading vs 13600K. Gaming performance was controversial at launch — AMD Ryzen 9600X dominated initially. Intel microcode updates improved things significantly. Better for content creation than initial reviews suggested. DDR5 only platform.
cons: No HT — fewer threads than predecessors

name: Core Ultra 7 265K
description: Arrow Lake's most balanced chip for enthusiasts. 24 cores (no HT so 24 threads) gives solid multi-core performance. The E-core cluster doubled, compensating for HT removal in parallel workloads. Intel ARC on-die improved over Meteor Lake. Power consumption at 159W PL2 is reasonable — a 240mm AIO handles it fine.

name: Core Ultra 9 285K
description: Intel's new flagship. 5.7 GHz boost clock, 36MB L3, NPU capable of running local AI inference (Copilot+ tasks). Initially lost to AMD Ryzen 9 9950X in benchmarks — Intel's microcode updates progressively improved this. Best use case for gaming where the NPU accelerates AI-powered features (DLSS 4, AI upscaling, background blur in calls). The new platform's AI capabilities are its primary differentiator.
cons: Lost initial benchmark comparisons vs AMD

name: Core Ultra 5 226V
description: Entry Lunar Lake. Despite only 8 cores, IPC improvements from the new Lion Cove P-core design make this surprisingly capable. The Xe2 GPU architecture is a significant leap over Xe — about 50% faster per core. At 17W TDP in a thin chassis, battery life stretches to 16+ hours real-world. RAM soldered on-package means no upgrades but much lower latency than DIMM slots.

name: Core Ultra 7 258V
description: The flagship Lunar Lake chip and Intel's most impressive laptop processor in years. The Arc 140V GPU competes with AMD's Radeon 890M in gaming benchmarks — remarkable for an ultrabook chip. 48 TOPS NPU runs Stable Diffusion, Whisper transcription, and Copilot+ features locally. Real-world battery: 16–20 hours on mixed use. Found in ASUS Zenbook S14, Dell XPS 13 9350.

name: Core Ultra 9 288V
description: Top Lunar Lake bin. 5.1 GHz boost on 17W base power is extraordinary engineering. Same core count as other Lunar Lake chips but higher frequency ceiling. The Arc 140V handles Lightroom exports, DaVinci Resolve playback, and DXVK gaming at 1080p medium-low settings reasonably well. A premium purchase for users who need Intel's ecosystem.
cons: Same 8 core count — CPU tasks not meaningfully faster than 258V

name: Xeon w5-3425
description: Entry workstation Xeon W. ECC DDR5 support is the critical differentiator — memory errors are corrected automatically, preventing crashes in critical engineering workloads. 4-channel DDR5 provides substantially more bandwidth than consumer dual-channel. AMX support means AI inference tasks are hardware-accelerated. Paired with professional GPUs (NVIDIA RTX 4000 Ada, AMD Radeon Pro W7700).
cons: Consumer CPUs beat it in gaming

name: Xeon w7-3465X
description: The top desktop workstation Xeon for most users. 28 cores, 56 threads, 8-channel DDR5 ECC, 75MB L3. Up to 4TB of RAM in a desktop workstation. AMX makes it capable of CPU-based ML inference without needing an accelerator card. Used in HP Z6 G5, Dell Precision 7960, Lenovo ThinkStation P7. ISV certified for AutoCAD, Siemens NX, CATIA, Ansys.

name: Xeon w9-3595X
description: The most powerful desktop workstation CPU ever sold. 60 P-cores, 120 threads, 112.5MB of L3 cache. A single w9-3595X in an HP Z8 workstation can replace a small render farm. AMX gives it legitimate ML inference acceleration — running INT8 matrix math at speeds that would have required a GPU cluster years ago. Used at major animation studios and semiconductor companies. Price: more than a mid-range car.
cons: EPYC beats it at server scale

name: Xeon 6 6731E
description: 96 E-cores at 205W — the density play. Each E-core is physically smaller than a P-core, allowing more cores per die. Cloud providers hosting hundreds of VMs per server need cores, not clock speed. At 205W for 96 cores, the performance-per-watt is exceptional for light parallel workloads. Azure and Alibaba Cloud have deployed these for cost-efficient VM fleets.

name: Xeon 6 9480P
description: 96 full P-cores on Intel 3. AMX and AVX-512 for AI inference acceleration. CXL 2.0 support for memory expansion. This is Intel's performance server flagship competing with AMD EPYC Turin. Used in Oracle Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Tencent Cloud for compute-intensive workloads. In two-socket configurations: 192 P-cores per server node.
cons: AMD EPYC Turin has more cores per socket

name: Ryzen 5 5600
description: The most beloved budget gaming CPU of the Zen 3 era. Released as a more affordable alternative to the 5600X with slightly lower clocks. Includes a Wraith Stealth cooler. On AM4 — meaning it works in older B450/X570 boards with BIOS updates, letting people upgrade without changing platforms. Still widely recommended for budget builds in 2024.

name: Ryzen 5 5600X
description: The 2020 gaming CPU of the year. The moment it launched, AMD reclaimed gaming CPU supremacy for the first time since Athlon 64. 19% IPC improvement over Zen 2 and competitive pricing. Ran circles around Intel's Comet Lake offerings. Still performs relevantly in 2024. The Zen 3 architecture's unified CCX (8 cores sharing same L3 pool) eliminated the latency penalty of Zen 2's split 4+4 design.
cons: Price dropped but so has relevance vs newer chips

name: Ryzen 7 5700X
description: 8 Zen 3 cores at 65W — remarkable efficiency. Runs cool enough for a 120mm AIO or even a large tower air cooler. For gaming, the 6-core 5600X is often faster (games prefer fewer, faster cores) but for video editing and streaming simultaneously, 8 cores makes a real difference. A very balanced chip that aged well.

name: Ryzen 7 5800X
description: The 5800X was criticized at launch for running hotter than expected and pricing that looked steep. It ran 90°C+ regularly under load, frustrating buyers. However its single-core performance was class-leading. The 5800X3D successor (with 3D V-Cache) transformed this chip's design into the gaming legend of its generation.

name: Ryzen 7 5800X3D
description: AMD's revolutionary 3D V-Cache technology debut. Extra 64MB of SRAM stacked directly on top of the CPU die via Through-Silicon Vias, giving 96MB total L3. In gaming benchmarks, it annihilated the i9-12900K despite lower clock speeds. Games are latency-bound — huge L3 cache keeps frequently accessed game data on-die. Still relevant in 2024. Cannot be overclocked (voltage sensitivity of cache stack).

name: Ryzen 9 5900X
description: 12 Zen 3 cores with two CCDs. In Blender, Premiere Pro, and V-Ray renders, this chip shocked the market with performance that matched or beat Intel's HEDT offerings at a fraction of the cost and power. 105W TDP with 64MB L3. A massive step forward for content creators on mainstream platforms. The chip that made Intel's Core X and AMD's own Threadripper look expensive for most creators.

name: Ryzen 9 5950X
description: The consumer desktop CPU that made HEDT irrelevant for most professionals. 16 Zen 3 cores in a mainstream AM4 socket at 105W was mind-bending. Competitive with Intel's 10th/11th Gen Core X HEDT chips costing twice as much. Two CCDs each with 8 cores and 32MB L3 (64MB total). Still used in professional workstations in 2024. The best AM4 CPU for content creation.

name: Ryzen 5 7600
description: Zen 4's mainstream gaming chip. 5.1 GHz boost from a 65W chip is remarkable. Now has an integrated GPU (RDNA 2 based) — you can technically run displays without a discrete GPU. AM5 platform future-proofs the system for Zen 5 and beyond. The iGPU is weak for gaming but great as a fallback. DDR5 makes the platform slightly more expensive than AM4.
cons: AM5 B650 boards expensive vs AM4 B550

name: Ryzen 5 7600X
description: Fastest 6-core gaming chip at launch. TSMC 5nm process delivers huge gains over Zen 3. Runs hotter than 7600 (105W vs 65W) but clocks higher. Undervolting via Curve Optimizer brings temps down dramatically while maintaining performance. AMD's new 5nm process also delivers substantially better performance on AVX-512 for developers.

name: Ryzen 7 7700X
description: 8 Zen 4 cores pushing to 5.4 GHz. Great all-rounder for gaming enthusiasts who also create content. Like the 7600X, undervolting is highly recommended — it drops temps significantly without losing performance. Pairs beautifully with an RTX 4070 or RX 7800 XT for a balanced high-end gaming rig.
cons: Thermal throttles at stock without good cooling

name: Ryzen 7 7800X3D
description: The best gaming CPU on the planet for most of 2023-2024. 3D V-Cache stacked on Zen 4 delivers 96MB L3. In CPU-bound games it can outperform the i9-14900K by 20-30%. Lower boost clock than 7700X (5.0 vs 5.4 GHz) but cache advantage more than compensates in games. Cannot overclock. Has iGPU as emergency fallback. The chip every serious gamer wants.
pros: Best gaming CPU of its generation

name: Ryzen 9 7900X
description: 12 Zen 4 cores with dual CCD design. 5.6 GHz all-core capable. In Blender, DaVinci Resolve, and Premiere, it's exceptional. AVX-512 support makes it a legitimate CPU-based ML inference machine. The 170W TDP means it runs noticeably hot — a 360mm AIO or quality tower cooler is necessary. Undervolting brings power down to 130W without performance loss.
cons: 7950X offers 4 more cores at modest premium

name: Ryzen 9 7950X
description: AMD's consumer desktop flagship. 16 Zen 4 cores, 5.7 GHz boost, 64MB L3. In Cinebench R23 multi-thread, it posted scores that would have required a Threadripper system just two years earlier. For creators who use Blender, C4D, After Effects, and Premiere simultaneously — this handles it all effortlessly. A compelling workstation replacement chip.

name: Ryzen 9 7950X3D
description: Unique asymmetric design: one CCD has 64MB 3D V-Cache (for gaming), the other runs without V-Cache at higher clocks (for compute). Windows scheduler routes gaming threads to V-Cache CCD, compute threads to the other. Result: near-7800X3D gaming FPS combined with near-7950X workstation performance in a single chip. 120W TDP is lower than 7950X (170W) despite matching it in compute.
cons: Not cheapest route to either gaming or creation separately

name: Ryzen 5 9600X
description: Zen 5's mainstream gaming chip. 5.4 GHz boost from 65W is excellent efficiency. The 16% IPC improvement over 9600 is noticeable in latency-sensitive games. However Intel's Core Ultra 5 245K with DDR5-7200 gave it real competition at launch. Best choice for 6-core gaming enthusiasts on a mid-range budget in 2024-2025.

name: Ryzen 7 9700X
description: 8 Zen 5 cores at just 65W — the efficiency ratio is stunning. AMD dropped TDP from 105W (7700X) to 65W while increasing performance. This means a cheap 120mm cooler works fine. The doubled AVX-512 throughput in Zen 5 also benefits developers running vectorized code, scientific workloads, and local AI models. Great all-around 2024-2025 chip.
cons: 65W can feel thermally limited in long sustained loads

name: Ryzen 7 9800X3D
description: The current best gaming CPU in the world by a significant margin. Zen 5 + 3D V-Cache with an improved thermal solution over earlier V-Cache chips. V-Cache is now placed underneath the core die (reversed from previous gen) allowing better heat dissipation and higher boost clocks. 5.2 GHz boost with 96MB L3 is a combination no competitor can match. Sold out at launch repeatedly — demand exceeded AMD's supply projections.
cons: Overkill if GPU is the bottleneck

name: Ryzen 9 9900X
description: 12 Zen 5 cores with doubled AVX-512 throughput makes this a legitimate AI inference CPU. Running quantized LLMs (LLaMA 3 8B, Mistral 7B) is faster on the 9900X than on a 7950X due to the wider vector units. Also a powerhouse for Blender, Premiere Pro, and code compilation. Lower TDP than 7900X (120W vs 170W) while performing better.
pros: Better AI inference than prev gen
cons: 9950X not much more for 4 extra cores

name: Ryzen 9 9950X
description: AMD's 2024 flagship consumer CPU. 16 Zen 5 cores, 5.7 GHz boost, doubled AVX-512. The most powerful consumer desktop CPU for workstation tasks in 2024. Local LLM inference is a compelling use case — quantized Llama 3 70B runs at 2–3 tokens/sec on this chip (CPU-only), fast enough for experimentation. In Blender, it matches server CPUs that cost 5× more. The AM5 iGPU means no discrete GPU needed for basic tasks.

name: Ryzen 9 9950X3D
description: Zen 5's ultimate expression: 16 cores, asymmetric 3D V-Cache (one CCD has V-Cache for gaming, one without for compute), 128MB total L3. Combines near-9800X3D gaming with near-9950X workstation performance. The scheduler intelligently routes workloads. If you game professionally AND do video editing/3D rendering as a job, this is the chip — there's nothing it can't handle well.
pros: Best gaming and work CPU simultaneously
cons: Most users better served by 9800X3D (gaming) or 9950X (work)

name: Ryzen AI 5 340
description: Entry Strix Point. Even at this tier, the Radeon 860M iGPU can handle CS2, Fortnite, and Valorant at 1080p medium settings. 50 TOPS NPU means Copilot+ features work. AMD's 4nm efficiency means thin, light, long-battery laptops. Budget Indian laptop market sees strong options at ₹55,000-70,000 with this chip.
cons: 10 cores but Zen 5c are smaller — heavy tasks feel it

name: Ryzen AI 7 350
description: The sweet spot of Strix Point. The Radeon 880M at 54W turbo can actually play Cyberpunk 2077 on low-medium 1080p acceptably. 12 cores for compilation, video editing, and heavy multi-tasking. Found in ASUS Vivobook S 15, Lenovo IdeaPad Pro 5. 50 TOPS NPU for AI. An incredibly complete package at the mid-range price.
cons: Needs 54W TDP mode for best GPU performance (fans spin hard)

name: Ryzen AI 9 HX 370
description: AMD's top Strix Point. The Radeon 890M with 16 CUs is genuinely impressive — AMD's claims of RTX 4060 competition in certain workloads are not exaggerated for the right scenarios. 50 TOPS NPU. 5.1 GHz boost. Found in ASUS ROG Zephyrus G14, ASUS ROG Zephyrus G16, HP OmniBook Ultra. If you want the best gaming without a bulky dGPU machine, this is it.
cons: dGPU variants still beat it for gaming

name: Ryzen AI Max+ 395 (Strix Halo)
description: AMD's most ambitious chip ever. 16 full Zen 5 P-cores (no Zen 5c compromise), 40 CU Radeon iGPU, and up to 128GB of LPDDR5X on a 256-bit memory bus — that's 256GB/s bandwidth, comparable to a discrete GPU. The GPU can run Cyberpunk at 1080p high settings. Local LLaMA 70B inference runs at 8–10 tokens/second — faster than any other laptop solution. Found in ASUS ROG Flow Z13, HP OmniBook Ultra Flip 14.

name: Threadripper 5960X
description: Entry to Zen 3 Threadripper. 24 cores, 128MB L3, quad-channel DDR4 ECC. Professional ECC memory support prevents silent data corruption in long rendering sessions. 280W TDP requires workstation-grade cooling (included platforms have purpose-built coolers). This is the chip animators, architects, and scientists use when they can't afford a full server but need more than a consumer desktop.

name: Threadripper 5980X
description: 64 Zen 3 cores at 280W — the desktop render monster of its era. 256MB of L3 cache across 8 CCDs. In Blender and V-Ray benchmarks it matched server systems costing 5× more. Used by VFX studios, game developers, and animation companies. The 5980X was the chip that made many small studios cancel their render farm subscriptions and bring rendering in-house.

name: Threadripper PRO 5965WX
description: PRO tier adds 8-channel DDR4 ECC (vs 4-channel on standard TR) and up to 2TB RAM. The RAM capacity alone makes this a different class of machine — holding an entire database or simulation dataset in memory eliminates storage bottlenecks. Found in HP Z8 G4 Fury workstations. ISV certified for Siemens NX, Ansys, and Dassault CATIA.

name: Threadripper PRO 5995WX
description: The most powerful professional workstation CPU of the Zen 3 era. 64 Zen 3 cores, 8-channel ECC DDR4, 256MB L3, 2TB RAM support, 128 PCIe 4.0 lanes. A single HP Z8 G4 Fury running two 5995WX processors contains 128 cores and can hold 4TB of RAM — effectively a small HPC cluster in a desktop chassis. Used at major financial institutions and film studios.

name: Threadripper 7960X
description: The entry to Zen 4 Threadripper. 24 Zen 4 cores running up to 5.3 GHz. DDR5 ECC quad-channel with up to 512GB RAM. 128 PCIe 5.0 lanes means you can run 4 × PCIe 5.0 ×16 GPUs simultaneously — perfect for multi-GPU AI training setups. The TRX50 platform is more accessible than PRO, with retail boards available. Substantial performance leap over 5960X.
pros: Better than prev gen at same price

name: Threadripper 7970X
description: 32 Zen 4 cores for the professional who needs more than 24 but doesn't need 64. 160MB L3 cache. The 4-CCD design gives excellent bandwidth. For architectural firms and VFX studios doing large-frame-count renders, the jump from 24 to 32 cores cuts render times proportionally. Still TRX50 platform so retail board availability.

name: Threadripper 7980X
description: The highest core-count retail workstation CPU available (at time of launch). 64 Zen 4 cores, 256MB L3, 350W TDP. In Cinebench R23 it posts scores rivaling two-socket Xeon systems. Animation studios use this to eliminate render farm costs. Eight Zen 4 CCDs stitched via Infinity Fabric to the IOD. The TRX50 platform allows 4 GPUs connected at full PCIe 5.0 ×16 speed each.

name: Threadripper PRO 7945WX
description: Entry PRO Threadripper 7000. The main reason to choose PRO over standard HEDT: 8-channel DDR5 ECC and up to 2TB RAM. 12 Zen 4 cores with extremely high single-core clocks (4.7 GHz base, 5.3 GHz boost). For engineers running Ansys simulations that require verified memory and massive datasets loaded in RAM, this is the minimum viable chip.
cons: 12 cores relatively few for workstation price

name: Threadripper PRO 7985WX
description: The pinnacle of professional desktop computing. 64 Zen 4 cores with 8-channel DDR5 ECC, 256MB L3, 2TB RAM capacity. Dual-socket WRX90 configurations can run TWO 7985WX processors — 128 cores, 4TB RAM, 512 PCIe 5.0 lanes in a single workstation. Found in HP Z8 Fury G5, Lenovo ThinkStation P8. ISV certified for Ansys, Dassault CATIA, Siemens NX, Autodesk Maya.

name: EPYC 7313P
description: Entry EPYC 7003. The 'P' suffix means single-socket optimized. 16 Zen 3 cores with 8-channel DDR4 ECC. Even this entry server chip has more memory bandwidth than the most powerful consumer desktop CPUs. 128MB L3. Powers small business servers, web hosting nodes, and entry database servers for companies moving off aging hardware.

name: EPYC 7763
description: The chip that launched EPYC's cloud dominance. 64 Zen 3 cores, 256MB L3, 8-channel DDR4, 280W. AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud all deployed massive 7763 fleets. In two-socket configs: 128 cores per server. When AWS launched their C6a and R6a instance types (EPYC 7763 powered), they offered 10% better price/performance than Intel equivalents. This chip is the reason Intel lost cloud market share dramatically.

name: EPYC 9224
description: Entry Genoa. Still has 12-channel DDR5 ECC — even the cheapest EPYC 9004 offers more memory bandwidth than any consumer workstation CPU. 24 Zen 4 cores. Used in 1U rack servers for companies migrating to DDR5 server infrastructure. The 200W TDP is very reasonable for a 24-core server CPU.

name: EPYC 9554P
description: Single-socket optimized EPYC flagship. The P-suffix chips get AMD's best binned dies for maximum single-socket performance. 64 Zen 4 cores, 256MB L3, 12-channel DDR5, up to 6TB RAM. This is the heart of many AWS, Azure, and Oracle Cloud instances running today. When you spin up a compute-optimized cloud VM, there's a significant chance you're on hardware like this.
cons: Only affordable at data center scale

name: EPYC 9654
description: 96 Zen 4 cores and 384MB L3 in a single package. 384MB of on-die cache is extraordinary — larger than the L3 of an entire server from 10 years ago. In dual-socket configs: 192 cores and 768MB of L3 per server node. AWS's m7a, r7a, and c7a instances run on EPYC 9654 or similar. Oracle Cloud's BM.HPC2.36 uses this chip. When you run a VM on a major cloud provider, this is likely what's underneath.
cons: Price requires data center economics to justify

name: EPYC 9754 (Bergamo)
description: 128 Zen 4c compact cores — the density play. More cores = more VMs per server = lower cost for cloud providers. Zen 4c cores are physically ~35% smaller than full Zen 4 P-cores, enabling 128 per chip. In single-socket servers: 128 cores / 256 threads. Dual-socket: 256 cores / 512 threads per server. Used by cloud providers offering high-density, lower-cost VM tiers.
cons: Lower per-core performance vs Genoa P-cores

name: EPYC 9355P
description: Entry Zen 5 EPYC. Even at entry level, 32 Zen 5 cores with 256MB L3 and 12-channel DDR5 ECC on SP5 is a generation ahead of anything a competitor offers. The 4.0 GHz boost is high for a server CPU — Zen 5's IPC improvements show up here. Drop-in upgrade on existing SP5 infrastructure from Genoa.

name: EPYC 9554P (Turin)
description: Turin's 64-core single-socket flagship. Higher base and boost clocks than Genoa equivalent due to Zen 5 efficiency. The doubled AVX-512 throughput dramatically accelerates AI inference workloads running on CPU — running INT8 transformer inference, embedding computations, and feature extraction is up to 2× faster vs EPYC 9554P Genoa. This is what makes Turin compelling for AI cloud infrastructure.
cons: Same price as Genoa — switch cost + power
Genoa still competitive in non-AI workloads

name: EPYC 9654P (Turin)
description: 96 Zen 5 cores with 384MB L3. The step up in per-core performance from Genoa equivalent is ~16% IPC + higher frequencies. For cloud providers, upgrading server nodes from Genoa 9654 to Turin 9654P delivers meaningful performance improvement with the same SP5 infrastructure. AVX-512 doubling makes this particularly attractive for AI inference at scale (running quantized transformer models across CPU clusters).
cons: Lower boost than single-socket P variants

name: EPYC 9965 (Turin Dense)
description: 192 cores per socket. 384 threads. 768MB of L3 cache in one processor. This is the highest core-count commercially available CPU ever made. 8 Zen 5c chiplets each with 24 cores on TSMC 3nm, stitched to an I/O die. At 500W, the performance-per-watt for AI inference workloads is unmatched. A dual-socket Turin Dense server has 384 cores, 768 threads, and 1.5TB of L3 cache — effectively a miniature supercomputer node. AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud are early adopters.
cons: 500W thermal load — specialized server cooling mandatory
Price only justifies at cloud economics scale
